items.passage:
Friction is the resistive force that arises whenever two surfaces slide, or attempt to slide, against each other. At the microscopic level, no surface is perfectly smooth; even polished metal contains tiny peaks and valleys called asperities. When two objects touch, these asperities interlock and cling together, so a force must be applied to break the contacts and produce motion.

This resistance takes two distinct forms depending on the state of motion. Static friction acts on a stationary object and must be overcome before sliding begins. [■] Once movement starts, kinetic friction takes over, and it is usually weaker than static friction. [■] This difference explains why a heavy box lurches forward the moment it finally breaks free. [■] Engineers measure both forms using a quantity known as the coefficient of friction, a ratio that depends on the two materials in contact. [■]

Because friction converts useful motion into heat, it wastes energy in most machinery. Designers therefore reduce it with lubricants and smooth bearings. However, friction is not merely a nuisance to be eliminated, since walking, braking, and gripping all depend on it, and any design that removes it entirely would create dangers of its own.
paragraphs: Friction is the resistive force that arises whenever two surfaces slide, or attempt to slide, against each other. At the microscopic level, no surface is perfectly smooth; even polished metal contains tiny peaks and valleys called asperities. When two objects touch, these asperities interlock and cling together, so a force must be applied to break the contacts and produce motion.

This resistance takes two distinct forms depending on the state of motion. Static friction acts on a stationary object and must be overcome before sliding begins. [■] Once movement starts, kinetic friction takes over, and it is usually weaker than static friction. [■] This difference explains why a heavy box lurches forward the moment it finally breaks free. [■] Engineers measure both forms using a quantity known as the coefficient of friction, a ratio that depends on the two materials in contact. [■]

Because friction converts useful motion into heat, it wastes energy in most machinery. Designers therefore reduce it with lubricants and smooth bearings. However, friction is not merely a nuisance to be eliminated, since walking, braking, and gripping all depend on it, and any design that removes it entirely would create dangers of its own.

questions.stem: The word "resistive" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: opposing
options.B: flexible
options.C: electrical
options.D: protective

questions.explanation: The passage defines friction as a force that "arises whenever two surfaces slide, or attempt to slide, against each other," so "resistive" describes a force that opposes motion.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does kinetic friction compare with static friction?

options.A: It acts only on stationary objects.
options.B: It is measured by a different coefficient entirely.
options.C: It is generally weaker than static friction.
options.D: It must be overcome before sliding begins.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that once movement starts, kinetic friction "is usually weaker than static friction."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about a surface that feels smooth to the touch?

options.A: It produces no friction at all when slid.
options.B: It still contains microscopic irregularities.
options.C: It is made only of polished metal.
options.D: It has no asperities to interlock.

questions.explanation: The passage notes that "no surface is perfectly smooth; even polished metal contains tiny peaks and valleys," implying a smooth-feeling surface still has microscopic asperities.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention a heavy box that "lurches forward" in paragraph 2?

options.A: To illustrate the difference between static and kinetic friction
options.B: To argue that heavy objects have no static friction
options.C: To show that lubricants make boxes slide easily
options.D: To describe how coefficients of friction are measured

questions.explanation: The box "lurches forward the moment it finally breaks free" is given as an everyday example of why static friction exceeds kinetic friction.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, rubber on dry concrete yields a high value, while steel on ice yields a very low one.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The inserted sentence gives concrete example values of the coefficient of friction, so it fits at slot 4, immediately after the sentence introducing the coefficient and its dependence on the two materials in contact.

items.passage:
A supply chain is the connected sequence of firms, activities, and resources that moves a product from raw materials to the final customer. It typically links suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, and retailers, each adding value at a distinct stage. Coordinating these independent parties is difficult, because each one pursues its own goals while depending on the timely performance of the others.

This coordination problem grows sharper as chains stretch across many countries. [■] Managers rely on forecasts of demand to decide how much to produce and stock, yet small errors in these forecasts multiply as they travel backward through the chain. [■] This amplification, known as the bullwhip effect, causes distant suppliers to swing between severe shortages and costly surpluses. [■] Sharing sales data across partners can dampen these swings, though firms are often reluctant to reveal sensitive information. [■]

Because modern chains are long and tightly optimized, they leave little slack to absorb sudden shocks. A strike, storm, or closed border at one node can halt production far downstream. Firms therefore weigh the savings of lean, single-source purchasing against the resilience of holding extra inventory. However, achieving both efficiency and robustness remains an elusive balance that no single strategy fully resolves.
paragraphs: A supply chain is the connected sequence of firms, activities, and resources that moves a product from raw materials to the final customer. It typically links suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, and retailers, each adding value at a distinct stage. Coordinating these independent parties is difficult, because each one pursues its own goals while depending on the timely performance of the others.

This coordination problem grows sharper as chains stretch across many countries. [■] Managers rely on forecasts of demand to decide how much to produce and stock, yet small errors in these forecasts multiply as they travel backward through the chain. [■] This amplification, known as the bullwhip effect, causes distant suppliers to swing between severe shortages and costly surpluses. [■] Sharing sales data across partners can dampen these swings, though firms are often reluctant to reveal sensitive information. [■]

Because modern chains are long and tightly optimized, they leave little slack to absorb sudden shocks. A strike, storm, or closed border at one node can halt production far downstream. Firms therefore weigh the savings of lean, single-source purchasing against the resilience of holding extra inventory. However, achieving both efficiency and robustness remains an elusive balance that no single strategy fully resolves.

questions.stem: The word "dampen" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: moisten
options.B: hide
options.C: reduce
options.D: delay

questions.explanation: The passage says sharing sales data can "dampen these swings," meaning it lessens or reduces the fluctuations of the bullwhip effect.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what causes the bullwhip effect?

options.A: The refusal of retailers to hold any inventory
options.B: The closing of borders between trading countries
options.C: The multiplication of forecast errors along the chain
options.D: The decision to buy from a single supplier

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "small errors in these forecasts multiply as they travel backward through the chain," and this amplification is named the bullwhip effect.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of supply chains EXCEPT

options.A: They link suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, and retailers.
options.B: They are simple for independent firms to coordinate.
options.C: They add value at distinct stages of production.
options.D: They can be disrupted by a shock at a single node.

questions.explanation: The passage says "Coordinating these independent parties is difficult," so it is not true that coordination is simple; the other three statements are all supported.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about firms that decline to share sales data?

options.A: They may experience larger swings between shortage and surplus.
options.B: They eliminate the bullwhip effect within their operations.
options.C: They always outperform firms that share information.
options.D: They face no risk from inaccurate demand forecasts.

questions.explanation: Since "sharing sales data across partners can dampen these swings," firms that are "reluctant to reveal sensitive information" would forgo that benefit and likely face larger swings.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For instance, a retailer that slightly overstates its needs prompts each upstream supplier to over-order even more.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The inserted sentence is a concrete illustration of amplified over-ordering, so it fits at slot 4, right after the bullwhip effect and the swings between shortages and surpluses have been introduced.

items.passage:
Large meteor impacts pose a rare but catastrophic threat, yet for much of the twentieth century geologists struggled to prove that any ancient crater had been carved by a falling body rather than a volcano. Both processes leave broad circular basins, and erosion erases the surface clues within a few million years, leaving the origin of many structures genuinely ambiguous.

The problem was resolved by looking below the surface for evidence that only an impact could create. [■] The enormous pressure of a collision deforms quartz grains along microscopic planes, producing a signature called shocked quartz. [■] Volcanic heat, however intense, cannot generate such pressures, so its presence became a reliable fingerprint. [■] Rare minerals that form only under sudden compression later reinforced the diagnosis. [■]

Armed with these criteria, researchers have confirmed roughly two hundred impact sites and linked the largest to mass extinctions. Yet the record remains badly incomplete, because most craters lie eroded, buried under sediment, or hidden beneath the oceans that cover the majority of the planet. Despite these advances, estimating how often catastrophic impacts truly occur remains uncertain, since the surviving evidence samples only a fraction of the events that history must have produced.
paragraphs: Large meteor impacts pose a rare but catastrophic threat, yet for much of the twentieth century geologists struggled to prove that any ancient crater had been carved by a falling body rather than a volcano. Both processes leave broad circular basins, and erosion erases the surface clues within a few million years, leaving the origin of many structures genuinely ambiguous.

The problem was resolved by looking below the surface for evidence that only an impact could create. [■] The enormous pressure of a collision deforms quartz grains along microscopic planes, producing a signature called shocked quartz. [■] Volcanic heat, however intense, cannot generate such pressures, so its presence became a reliable fingerprint. [■] Rare minerals that form only under sudden compression later reinforced the diagnosis. [■]

Armed with these criteria, researchers have confirmed roughly two hundred impact sites and linked the largest to mass extinctions. Yet the record remains badly incomplete, because most craters lie eroded, buried under sediment, or hidden beneath the oceans that cover the majority of the planet. Despite these advances, estimating how often catastrophic impacts truly occur remains uncertain, since the surviving evidence samples only a fraction of the events that history must have produced.

questions.stem: The word "ambiguous" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: ancient
options.B: circular
options.C: unclear
options.D: shallow

questions.explanation: The passage says erosion erases the surface clues, "leaving the origin of many structures genuinely ambiguous," meaning the origin was uncertain or unclear.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why is shocked quartz a reliable indicator of an impact?

options.A: It forms whenever quartz is heated by lava.
options.B: It appears in every eroded circular basin.
options.C: It is easily seen on the eroded surface.
options.D: It requires pressures that volcanoes cannot produce.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "Volcanic heat, however intense, cannot generate such pressures," so the presence of shocked quartz points uniquely to an impact.

questions.stem: What does "its" refer to in the phrase "its presence became a reliable fingerprint" in paragraph 2?

options.A: volcanic heat
options.B: the collision
options.C: shocked quartz
options.D: the pressure

questions.explanation: The preceding sentence introduces "shocked quartz" as the signature of impact pressure; it is the presence of shocked quartz that serves as the reliable fingerprint.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about the number of confirmed impact sites?

options.A: It probably underestimates the true number of past impacts.
options.B: It includes every large crater on the planet.
options.C: It has been fixed at two hundred for a century.
options.D: It counts mainly craters found beneath the oceans.

questions.explanation: Because "the surviving evidence samples only a fraction of the events" and most craters are eroded, buried, or under the oceans, the confirmed total likely understates the true number.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because this deformation survives long after the crater itself has vanished, it can be read in rock samples millions of years later.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The inserted sentence elaborates on the durability of the quartz deformation just introduced, so it fits at slot 2, immediately after the sentence that defines shocked quartz.

items.passage:
A habit is an automatic behavior that the brain performs with little conscious effort after it has been repeated many times. Psychologists describe habits as arising from a simple loop of three parts: a cue that triggers the action, the routine itself, and a reward that reinforces it. Over time this loop becomes so efficient that the mind barely registers the behavior at all.

This efficiency has a clear biological basis. When a routine is new, regions of the brain associated with deliberate decision-making are highly active, monitoring each step. As repetition strengthens the underlying neural pathways, control gradually shifts to deeper structures that run the routine automatically. This shift frees conscious attention for other tasks, which is why an experienced driver can navigate a familiar route while holding a conversation.

Because habits bypass conscious control, they are notoriously hard to break by willpower alone. Researchers therefore recommend altering the cue or substituting a new routine that delivers a similar reward, rather than simply resolving to stop. However, even these strategies may fail under stress, when old cues reassert themselves and the original routine tends to return before a new one is fully established.
paragraphs: A habit is an automatic behavior that the brain performs with little conscious effort after it has been repeated many times. Psychologists describe habits as arising from a simple loop of three parts: a cue that triggers the action, the routine itself, and a reward that reinforces it. Over time this loop becomes so efficient that the mind barely registers the behavior at all.

This efficiency has a clear biological basis. When a routine is new, regions of the brain associated with deliberate decision-making are highly active, monitoring each step. As repetition strengthens the underlying neural pathways, control gradually shifts to deeper structures that run the routine automatically. This shift frees conscious attention for other tasks, which is why an experienced driver can navigate a familiar route while holding a conversation.

Because habits bypass conscious control, they are notoriously hard to break by willpower alone. Researchers therefore recommend altering the cue or substituting a new routine that delivers a similar reward, rather than simply resolving to stop. However, even these strategies may fail under stress, when old cues reassert themselves and the original routine tends to return before a new one is fully established.

questions.stem: The word "triggers" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: prompts
options.B: delays
options.C: rewards
options.D: records

questions.explanation: A cue that "triggers the action" is one that starts or prompts the behavior, matching the loop's role of setting the routine in motion.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what are the three parts of the habit loop?

options.A: A pathway, a structure, and a decision
options.B: A cue, a routine, and a reward
options.C: A trigger, a driver, and a conversation
options.D: A repetition, an effort, and a memory

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 lists the loop as "a cue that triggers the action, the routine itself, and a reward that reinforces it."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about behaviors that are still new?

options.A: They are run entirely by deeper brain structures.
options.B: They never engage conscious decision-making.
options.C: They free attention for other tasks immediately.
options.D: They demand more conscious attention than practiced ones.

questions.explanation: The passage says that for a new routine, decision-making regions are "highly active, monitoring each step," implying new behaviors require more conscious attention than automatic ones.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention an experienced driver in paragraph 2?

options.A: To illustrate how automatic routines free up conscious attention
options.B: To warn that driving habits are dangerous to form
options.C: To argue that conversation strengthens neural pathways
options.D: To show that new routines require little monitoring

questions.explanation: The driver example follows the claim that the shift to automatic control "frees conscious attention for other tasks," illustrating that point.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are recommended or true ways to change a habit EXCEPT

options.A: altering the cue that triggers the routine
options.B: substituting a routine with a similar reward
options.C: relying on willpower alone to stop it
options.D: recognizing that stress can undermine the effort

questions.explanation: The passage says habits are "notoriously hard to break by willpower alone" and recommends changing cues or routines instead, so relying on willpower alone is not an endorsed method.

items.passage:
Population aging is the process by which the median age of a society rises as the proportion of older people grows relative to the young. It results from two long-term trends acting together: falling birth rates, which shrink the number of children, and rising life expectancy, which increases the number of elderly. Demographers regard it as one of the defining social transformations of the present century.

The mechanism behind this shift can be pictured through the dependency ratio, a measure comparing people outside the working ages with those within them. As families have fewer children and adults live longer, the share of working-age people supporting each dependent gradually declines. A smaller workforce must therefore fund the pensions, healthcare, and services required by a growing older population, straining public budgets that were designed for a younger society.

The significance of this trend extends well beyond economics. Aging reshapes labor markets, housing demand, and the political weight of different generations, and no government has yet fully adapted its institutions to it. However, whether aging societies can sustain their commitments without either raising the retirement age or admitting more migrants remains a contested question that current policies only partly address.
paragraphs: Population aging is the process by which the median age of a society rises as the proportion of older people grows relative to the young. It results from two long-term trends acting together: falling birth rates, which shrink the number of children, and rising life expectancy, which increases the number of elderly. Demographers regard it as one of the defining social transformations of the present century.

The mechanism behind this shift can be pictured through the dependency ratio, a measure comparing people outside the working ages with those within them. As families have fewer children and adults live longer, the share of working-age people supporting each dependent gradually declines. A smaller workforce must therefore fund the pensions, healthcare, and services required by a growing older population, straining public budgets that were designed for a younger society.

The significance of this trend extends well beyond economics. Aging reshapes labor markets, housing demand, and the political weight of different generations, and no government has yet fully adapted its institutions to it. However, whether aging societies can sustain their commitments without either raising the retirement age or admitting more migrants remains a contested question that current policies only partly address.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: How the dependency ratio is calculated by demographers
options.B: What population aging is and why it challenges societies
options.C: Why raising the retirement age reduces public spending
options.D: How falling birth rates increase national life expectancy

questions.explanation: The passage defines population aging, explains its mechanism through the dependency ratio, and discusses its broad social significance and challenges.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, which two trends together produce population aging?

options.A: Falling birth rates and rising life expectancy
options.B: Rising birth rates and increased migration
options.C: Higher retirement ages and shrinking workforces
options.D: Growing pensions and expanding healthcare demand

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states aging results from "falling birth rates... and rising life expectancy" acting together.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It disputes the definition of aging given in paragraph 1.
options.B: It introduces an unrelated demographic problem.
options.C: It summarizes the political effects mentioned earlier.
options.D: It explains the mechanism behind the process paragraph 1 defines.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 defines population aging, and paragraph 2 opens with "The mechanism behind this shift," explaining how it works through the dependency ratio.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about public budgets in aging societies?

options.A: They were built on assumptions that aging undermines.
options.B: They automatically expand as workforces shrink.
options.C: They were designed to support a growing elderly share.
options.D: They face no strain from rising healthcare costs.

questions.explanation: The passage says a smaller workforce strains "public budgets that were designed for a younger society," implying their founding assumptions are undermined by aging.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what does population aging affect beyond economics?

options.A: Only the pensions paid to retired workers
options.B: Solely the birth rates of younger families
options.C: Labor markets, housing demand, and generational political weight
options.D: Just the migration policies of neighboring nations

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states aging "reshapes labor markets, housing demand, and the political weight of different generations."
